concepts.title: Using Binomial Expansion Series
concepts.conceptBody:
Division of identical objects

Number of ways to divide $\text{n}$ identical things in $\text{r}$ groups.

Result 1: Such that each group gets zero or more items $={{~}^{\text{n}+\text{r}-1}}{{\mathbf{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}$

(empty groups are allowed)

Result 2: such that each group gets atleast one item $={{~}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\mathbf{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}$

(empty groups are not allowed)

Result 3: such that groups contain atleast p items & atmost q items $\to $ follow "PROCEDURE" let us see how to solve questions of these types.

Q) How can we divide 8 identical balls among three boys that each get atleast two balls? 

Solution: 

Let us solve this question by Integral equation method{PROCEDURE$\}$

let

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {{x}_{1}}\text{ be }\text{ the }\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ balls }\text{ given }\text{ to }\text{ Boy }1 \\ {{x}_{2}}\text{ be }\text{ the }\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ balls }\text{ given }\text{ to }\text{ Boy }2 \\ {{x}_{3}}\text{ be }\text{ the }\text{ number }\text{ of }\text{ balls }\text{ given }\text{ to }\text{ Boy }3 \\ \end{array}$

So, assuming this equation becomes

${{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}+{{\text{x}}_{3}}=8$ (i)

as there are a total of 8 balls.

Also, condition defined by question (CDQ)

${{x}_{1}}\ge 2,{{x}_{2}}\ge 2,{{x}_{3}}\ge 2\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{. }\text{ atleast }2\text{ balls }\text{ to }\text{ be }\text{ given }\text{ to }\text{ each }\text{ child}\text{. }$

now we will find the logical constraint (L.C.), i.e. the maximum balls each boy can get

$\begin{array}{l} \therefore \text{ Max}\text{. }\left[ {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right] & =8-\text{Min}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right]-\text{Min}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{3}} \right] \\ {} & =8-2-2=4 \\ \end{array}$

Since there are same constraints on ${{x}_{2}}\And {{x}_{3}}$

$\Rightarrow $ i.e. $\Rightarrow \text{ Max}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}} \right]=4,\text{i}=1,2,3$

So, a boy can have a minimum of 2 balls and a maximum of 4 balls.

$\therefore $ Number of solutions of the equation.

$=$ coefficient of ${{x}^{8}}$ in the expansion

$=$ coeff. of ${{x}^{8}}$ in ${{\left( {{x}^{2}}+{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{4}} \right)}^{3}}$

= coeff. of ${{x}^{8}}$ in ${{x}^{6}}{{\left( 1+x+{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{3}}$

$=$ coeff. of ${{x}^{2}}$ in ${{\left( 1+x+{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{3}}$

$=$ coeff. of ${{x}^{2}}$ in ${{\left[ \frac{1-{{x}^{3}}}{1-x} \right]}^{3}}$

= coeff. of ${{x}^{2}}$ in ${{\left[ 1-{{x}^{3}} \right]}^{3}}{{(1-x)}^{-3}}$

= coeff. of ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$ in $\left[ ~_{1}^{3}{{\text{C}}_{0}} \right.$ ' $\left. +{{~}^{3}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{3}}+{{~}^{3}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{6}}-{{~}^{3}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}^{9}} \right]{{(1-\text{x})}^{-3}}$

only this term will be able to give ${{x}^{2}}$ term from ${{(1-x)}^{-3}}$ as other terms are of greater power than ${{x}^{2}}$

$\therefore$ Solution $=$ coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$ in ${{~}^{3}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(1-\text{x})}^{-3}}$

$={{~}^{3+2-1}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}={{~}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}=6$

Tip: in the expression of ${{(1-x)}^{-n}}={{a}_{0}}+{{a}_{1}}x+{{a}_{2}}{{x}^{2}}+\ldots \infty $

coefficient of $\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}}=$ ${ }^{\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{r}-1} \mathbf{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$

(take $\text{n}$ positive in the formula)

Trick 

To get rid of 1 expansion and reducing our calculation use this method.

then max value = CDQ i.e. expansion take max value = ∞ i.e. expansion will become

will be $\left( 1+x+{{x}^{2}}+\ldots {{x}^{n}} \right)$ $\left( 1+x+{{x}^{2}}\ldots \infty \right)=\frac{1}{1-x}$ forget CDQ here

where $n$ is defined by $\text{CDQ}$

for ex. take the previous illustration only

condition defined by question: 

$x_i \geq 2$ $\mathrm{i}=1,2,3$

$\And $ no mention of maximum value so we can take that to $\infty $

Logical constraint 

$x_i \leq 4$ $\mathrm{i}=1,2,3$

Now you can see

$\text{CDQ}>4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }(\infty >4)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ we can take the expansion $\left( {{x}^{2}}+{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{4}} \right)$ as $\left( {{x}^{2}}+{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{4}}+\ldots \infty \right)$

so now questions become

= coeff. of $x^8$ in $\left(x^2+x^3+x^4+\ldots \infty\right)^3$

= of $x^8$ in $x^6\left(1+x^2+x^2 \ldots \infty\right)$

$=\frac{x^6}{(1-x)}$

= coeff. of $x^2$ in $(1-x)^{-3}$

exactly what we have done earlier, but saves us from the trouble of solving ${{(1-\text{x})}^{3}}$.

Q) Find the number of integral solutions for the equation ${{x}_{1}}+{{x}_{2}}+{{x}_{3}}=10$ where $2\le {{x}_{i}}\le 4$ ? 

Solution: 

Constraint defined by question

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{Min}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}} \right]=2 \\ {} & \text{Max}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}} \right]=4 \\ \end{array}$

Now we will find logical constraint

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{Max}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right] & ~=10-\text{Min}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right]-\text{Min}\left[ {{\text{x}}_{3}} \right] \\ {} & ~=10-2-2 \\ {} & ~=6 \\ \end{array}$

Now CDQ < LC

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ we cannot expand the series to $\infty $.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Solution $=$ coeff. of ${{\text{x}}^{10}}$ in $\underbrace{\left(x^2+x^3+x^4\right)^3}_{\text {because } 2 \leq x_i \leq 4}$

Or coeff. of $x^{10}$ in $x^6\left(1+x+x^2\right)^3$

Or coeff. of $x^4$ in $\left(1+x+x^2\right)^3$

coeff. of $x^4$ in $\left(1-x^3\right)^3(1-x)^{-3}$

coeff. of $\mathrm{x}^4$ in $\left.\left({ }^1 \mathrm{C}_0-{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{x}^3\right)+{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{x}^6-{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{x}^9\right)(1-\mathrm{x})^{-3}$

only these 2 terms will be able to give the term ${{x}^{4}}$ as ${{(1-x)}^{-3}}=1+x+{{x}^{2}}+\ldots \infty $

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Answer $={ }^3 C_0 \quad$ coeff. of $x^4$ in $(1-x)^{-3}-{ }^3 C_1 \quad$ coeff. of $x^1$ in $(1-x)^{-3}$

$={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_0 \quad{ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4-{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \quad{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1$

$=15-9$

$=6$

concepts.title: Using Mapping Method
concepts.conceptBody:
Mapping method

What if we can somehow remove the part of solving expansion series and can directly use formulas for dividing things such that zero or more and atleast one items be used.

Yes, we can but only when

There is NO maximum constraint on any of the variable, in the question.

Let us understand with the help of an illustration.

Q) How many integral solutions are there to $x+y+z+w=29$, where $x\ge 1,y\ge 2,z\ge 3$ and $\text{W}\ge 0$ ? 

Solution: 

For applying Mapping Method

STEP 1: Check if there is any maximum constraint on any variable.

If yes $\to $ we cannot apply this method

If no $\to $ we can go to step 2

STEP 2: In this step we will try to bring each variable to a position where we can give zero or more elements to it rather than the min constraint defines by question.

We do this step, so that we can use the direct formula (Result-1)

Now $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\ge 1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}\ge 2,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ z}\ge 3,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ w}\ge 0$

if we replace

$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{X}+1 \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$ can take 0 or more

$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{Y}+2 \rightarrow$ similarly $\mathrm{Y}$ can take 0 or more

$\left.\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{z}=\mathrm{Z}+3 \rightarrow \\ \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{W}+0 \rightarrow\end{array}\right\}$similarly z & w

i.e. Old value $=$ new value + Min. (old value)

putting these values in equation $\text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}+\text{w}=29$ it becomes

$(\mathrm{X}+1)+(\mathrm{Y}+2)+(\mathrm{Z}+3)+(\mathrm{W}+0)=29$

$\Rightarrow$ $\underbrace{\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{Z}+\mathrm{W}}=23$

here each variable can have 0 or more items

$\Rightarrow$ using result 1, no. of solutions $={ }^{23+4-1} \mathrm{C}_{4-1}$

$={ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_3$

Q) Find the number of non-negative integral solutions of $x+y+z+4t=20$ 

Solution: 

First of all, note that we have to find non-negative solutions i.e. each variable can take 0 or more values.

Now let us see how to solve these types of questions.

Rewriting the equation as

$\text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}=20-4\text{t}$ (i)

now the sum of $x,y,z$ will vary according to the value of $t$. Note here that all variable is non-negative so L.H.S. cannot take negative values.

adding all of them will give us the final result.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total number of integral solutions

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~={{~}^{22}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{18}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{14}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{6}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{2}}{{\text{C}}_{2}} \\ {} & ~=536 \\ \end{array}$

Now we will attempt upgraded version of this question.

Q) Find the number of non-negative integral solutions of $2{{x}_{1}}+3{{x}_{2}}+{{x}_{3}}=20$. 

Solution: 

Now this is different & difficult from the question done above. So how we go about it?

Rule: Shift highest coefficient to R.H.S. i.e. $3{{\text{x}}_{2}}$ in this question. The logic is simple, you will subtract bigger term & hence the number of cases will be less. Let us solve it to understand it better.

So, equation becomes

$2 x_1+x_3=20-3 x_2$

$\therefore \quad$ Total ways $=11+9+8+6+5+3+2$ = 44

concepts.title: Solving Integral Inequations
concepts.conceptBody:
Solving Integral Inequations

We will explain the procedure of this type by taking an example.

Q) Find the positive integral solutions of the inequality $x+y+z\le 10$ 

Solution: 

Again, the first step is to note that we are asked to find positive integral solutions i.e. $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$ can takes values $\ge 1$ but not zero.

Now come to inequality part

$\text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}\le 10$

Then there is no loss in writing

$\text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}+\text{w}=10$

where $\text{w}$ is a non-negative (i.e. can also take 0) integer.

But now x, y, z are positive integers & w is non negative integer. Making all of them non-negative integer using mapping method.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{x}=\text{x}-1 \\ {} & \text{y}=\text{y}-1 \\ {} & \text{z}=\text{z}-1 \\ {} & \text{w}=\text{w} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Equation becomes

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+\left( \text{y}+1 \right)+\left( \text{z}+1 \right)+\text{w}=10 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}+\text{y}+\text{z}+\text{w}=7 \\ \end{array}$

This is just dividing 7 objects into 4 groups where each group contain zero or more items.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total ways $={{~}^{7+4-1}}{{\text{C}}_{4-1}}={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}=120$

examples.title: In how many ways can 5 prizes be distributed among four students when every student can take one or more prizes
In how many ways can 10 balls be divided between two boys, one receiving two and the other eight balls
In how many ways 3 letters can be posted in 4 letter-boxes, if all the letters are not posted in the same letter-box
The number of ways in which 9 persons can be divided into three equal groups
Choose the correct number of ways in which 15 different books can be divided into five heaps of equal number of books
The number of ways of dividing 52 cards amongst four players equally, are
